title: The Emperor's Last Breath
content:
The sky turned red over the Celestial Palace.

Not the red of dawn or sunset, but the deep, angry crimson of spilled blood. The nine sons of the Dragon Emperor felt it before they saw it — a tremor running through the foundations of heaven itself, as if the world had just cracked down its spine.

Long Jie was in the eastern gardens when the sky changed. He'd been watching a mortal butterfly struggle against a spider's web, its wings beating so fast they blurred. The spider was patient. The butterfly was desperate. Jie had seen this play out a hundred times before, but today something made him stop.

Then the sky bled red, and his blood turned cold.

"Your father is dying." The words came from the spider. Jie didn't flinch. He'd long stopped being surprised when insects spoke to him — such was the nature of being the ninth son, the one born closest to the mortal realm, the one who heard things his brothers couldn't.

He left the butterfly to its fate and ran.

---

The Hall of Eternal Ascension was packed when Jie arrived. All eight of his brothers stood in a semicircle around the dragon throne, their faces carved from stone and ice. The Emperor lay slumped across his seat of power, scales the color of faded gold, breath coming in wet rattles that echoed off the jade walls.

"Finally decided to show up, little brother." Long Huang didn't bother turning around. The firstborn's voice carried the weight of a mountain — deep, immovable, absolute. "Too busy playing with insects?"

Jie didn't answer. He found his place at the end of the line, where he always stood. The position of smallest, weakest, least significant.

The Emperor's eyes opened.

They were the eyes that had once burned entire kingdoms to ash. Now they were milky, unfocused, swimming with the haze of approaching death. But when they found Jie, something flickered. Recognition. Perhaps even affection.

"Jie." The Emperor's voice was barely a whisper. "Come closer."

His brothers shifted. Huang's jaw tightened. Second-born Long Yan let out a low growl. But no one moved to stop him.

Jie knelt beside his father. The old dragon's breath smelled of smoke and endings.

"Don't let them..." The Emperor coughed, scales rattling. "Don't let them forget what it means to be human."

"Father, you need rest—"

"No time." A clawed hand gripped Jie's wrist with surprising strength. "You have their compassion. You have their weakness. But you also have their strength, if you're brave enough to learn it."

The grip went slack. Jie watched the light leave his father's eyes, watched the Dragon Emperor of Ten Thousand Years become nothing more than meat and bone and fading warmth.

The silence lasted exactly three breaths.

Then Long Huang stepped forward, shouldering Jie aside so hard he stumbled into Third Brother Long Cheng. "The Emperor is dead. The seal is broken."

He reached for the crown.

A scroll of golden light materialized between his fingers, burning with characters that made the air itself tremble. The Emperor's final decree. Huang's face went pale as he read it, then red, then a shade of purple that made Jie think of bruised plums.

"What does it say?" Long Yan demanded.

Huang's knuckles whitened as he crushed the scroll. It reformed instantly, floating above his palm, refusing to be destroyed.

He read aloud through gritted teeth:

"Let it be known: I, Long Wei, Dragon Emperor of the Nine Heavens, do hereby declare that the succession to my throne shall be decided not by birthright, but by worth. The Dragon Ascension Trials shall commence at the next new moon. Three tests across three realms. The victor shall be crowned emperor. All sons must compete. None may refuse."

Silence.

Then laughter.

It started with Long Yan, the second son, whose flames of rage were legendary. His laugh was a forge fire, hot and cruel. Third brother Long Cheng joined in, his voice like grinding stone. Fourth brother Long Zhan, the warrior, barked out something between a laugh and a snort.

Soon all eight were laughing.

Jie stood alone in the center of their mockery, feeling small. Feeling human.

"You?" Huang finally managed, wiping tears of mirth from his eyes. "You think you can compete? You barely held a transformation at two hundred years. Your flame is so weak it can't even light a candle. You spend your days talking to spiders and butterflies and mortal beggars."

He stepped forward, close enough that Jie could smell the wine on his breath.

"You're not a dragon, little brother. You're a mistake. A runt. The Emperor should have drowned you at birth like the failed hatchling you are."

Jie's hands clenched into fists. His nails bit into his palms. But he said nothing.

He had learned, in two hundred years of being the smallest, the weakest, the least, that words were weapons that cut both ways. And he had learned something else, something none of his brothers understood.

He had learned to listen.

"Three months," Jie said quietly.

Huang blinked. "What?"

"Until the new moon. Three months." He looked up, meeting his brother's burning gaze. "I'll be ready."

The laughter started again, but Jie was already walking away.

---

The mortal realm was cold.

Jie had forgotten how cold. The Celestial Palace existed outside of seasons, outside of weather, outside of the petty discomforts that plagued the lower world. But here, in the outskirts of a village called Jade Hollow, winter bit hard enough to make even a dragon's blood run thin.

He'd left his dragon form behind. Too conspicuous. Too much of a target. In the shape of a man — young, lean, unremarkable — he walked through streets packed with frozen mud and the smell of burning dung.

A woman was crying outside a broken house. Her children huddled behind her, faces pinched with hunger.

Jie had nothing to give them. He had left the Celestial Palace with only the clothes on his back, refusing to take anything his brothers could claim was unfair advantage.

But he stopped.

"Why do you weep?" he asked.

The woman looked up, startled by his accent, the strange cadence of his speech. "Bandits," she said. "They took our winter stores. My husband went after them. He hasn't returned."

Jie looked at the broken door. At the children. At the woman's hands, cracked and bleeding from work that had never quite earned enough.

In the Celestial Palace, mortals were abstractions. Stories told to frighten hatchlings. Legends of suffering and struggle and brief, bright lives that burned out like sparks.

But this woman was real. Her pain was real. And Jie realized, standing in the frozen mud of a nameless village, that he had no idea how to help her.

He was two hundred years old. He could speak the language of spiders and hear the whispers of wind spirits. He could transform into a dragon the size of a house and breathe flames that could melt stone.

But he didn't know how to help a crying woman feed her children.

"Where are the bandits?" he asked.

The woman pointed toward the forest. "The Blackwood. But you can't—"

Jie was already gone.

---

The Blackwood was dark, the kind of dark that swallowed light before it could touch the ground. Jie moved through it in his human form, stepping on fallen leaves that should have crackled but somehow didn't.

He found the bandit camp by following the smell of roasting meat.

There were twelve of them. Hard men, scarred and rough, sitting around a fire that was too large for their needs. They had taken more than just the woman's stores — Jie saw piles of stolen goods, bags of grain, tools, jewelry torn from dead hands.

He also saw the woman's husband. Tied to a tree. Still alive, but barely.

Jie stepped into the firelight.

The bandits saw a boy. Young. Thin. Dressed in strange silks that were already torn and dirty. Unarmed.

They laughed.

"You lost, boy?" The leader stood, a knife appearing in his hand like it had always been there. "This ain't a place for children."

"I'm not a child," Jie said. "I'm two hundred years old."

More laughter. The leader stepped closer, knife glinting. "And I'm the Jade Emperor himself. Now run along before I skin you and add your hide to my collection."

Jie looked at the knife. At the fire. At the stolen goods piled high.

He remembered his father's words: *You have their strength, if you're brave enough to learn it.*

He didn't know how to fight. Dragons didn't need to — they had claws and fire and raw power. But he couldn't use that here. Couldn't reveal what he was. The mortals would panic, the news would spread, and his brothers would know exactly where to find him.

So he would have to learn.

"Teach me," he said.

The bandit leader's brow furrowed. "Teach you what?"

"How to fight. How to survive. How to be..." Jie searched for the word. "Human."

The silence that followed was broken by the crackling of the fire.

The bandit leader laughed, but it was different now. Less mockery, more curiosity. "You're either the bravest fool I've ever met, or the stupidest. Either way, this'll be entertaining."

He tossed his knife at Jie's feet.

"Pick it up, boy. Let's see what you've got."

Jie bent down. The knife was heavier than it looked, the grip worn smooth by years of use. He held it awkwardly, the blade pointed at nothing in particular, his stance all wrong.

The bandit leader smiled.

It was not a kind smile.

But Jie didn't need kindness. He needed to learn. And if this was the price of knowledge, he would pay it.

Behind him, in the Celestial Palace, his eight brothers were preparing for the Dragon Ascension Trials. They were training in ancient arts, forging weapons, making alliances, doing everything dragons had done for ten thousand years.

Jie was in a muddy camp, holding a knife he didn't know how to use, facing a man who wanted to kill him.

And for the first time in his life, he felt like he was exactly where he was supposed to be.

title: The Weight of Scales
content:
The Celestial Arena stretched across the eastern sky like a wound split open between clouds. Long Jie stood at its edge, his bare feet pressed against stone that hummed with the trapped heat of a thousand suns. Below him, the mortal world spun in lazy blue and green, so far down that the great rivers looked like veins on a sleeping giant's hand.

Six of his brothers had already claimed their positions around the arena's rim. Each one a different shape of power.

Long Yin, the eldest, had taken the form of a coiled jade serpent three times the height of a man, his scales etched with characters of law and judgment. When he breathed, mist curled from his nostrils like the exhalation of mountains. He did not look at Jie. He never looked at Jie.

Long Feng stood opposite his twin, his flames painting shadows across the stone. Fire licked between his teeth, turning his words to smoke. "Still wearing mortal skin, little brother? The Trials begin today, and you come dressed as a man."

Jie touched his own face. Fingers against cheek. Flesh and bone. No scales. No horns. No fire.

"The Emperor's blood runs thin," said Long Shan from somewhere above. He had taken sky form, a dragon of storm clouds and thunder, his voice rumbling through the air like distant war drums. "Perhaps our mother loved a human too well. Perhaps the youngest is not a dragon at all."

The other brothers laughed. Six voices. Six kinds of cruelty.

Jie did not answer. He had learned, in his sixteen years of life, that words were weapons his brothers wielded better than he ever could. They had centuries of practice. He had only the memory of his mother's hands brushing his hair back from his forehead, the way she would whisper, "You have my eyes, little one. Your father said that was a gift."

She had died when he was seven. The Emperor had not come to her funeral.

The Seventh Brother, Long Xu, drifted closer on currents of shadow. He was the quiet one, the one who watched from corners, whose form was never quite solid. "The trials begin at noon," he said, his voice like paper sliding across stone. "Father's steward will announce the first test."

"Father's steward is dead," Long Yin said, unwinding his great body with a sound like grinding jade. "The Emperor's heart stopped beating three days ago. The steward followed him into the void, as is the custom."

The arena fell silent.

Jie felt it then. A shift in the air. A pressure change, like before a typhoon. Something had been broken. Something that could not be fixed with fire or law or the weight of jade coils.

"Then who announces?" Long Feng asked, his flames flickering low.

Long Yin's head turned slowly. His eyes, black and gold, found Jie for the first time in years.

"The youngest holds the Emperor's final decree."

Jie's blood turned to ice.

"I have no such thing."

"You do." Long Yin's voice carried no warmth, no accusation. Simply fact. "Father summoned you on his deathbed. The servants saw you enter. They saw you leave. You carry something beneath your ribs, little brother. The question is whether you will open your hands or keep them closed."

Every eye fixed on him. Six brothers. Six sets of teeth and claws and judgment.

Jie remembered that night. The Emperor's chambers had smelled of sandalwood and something rotting beneath. His father had looked small, smaller than Jie had ever imagined the Celestial Dragon could look. Skin like old parchment stretched across bones that had once held up the sky.

"Come closer, boy."

Jie had approached. Had taken the withered hand when it reached for him. Had felt the cold of death already settling into those fingers.

"Your mother," the Emperor had whispered, "was the only good thing I ever did. And I did not deserve her."

Then the Emperor had pressed something into Jie's palm. Something that burned. Something that crawled up his arm and settled behind his heart like a second pulse.

"Hide it," the Emperor had said. "Hide it until you understand what it means. Then decide."

The Emperor had died before Jie could ask what the thing was. Before he could ask anything at all.

Now, standing in the Celestial Arena with his brothers circling like sharks, Jie understood that whatever his father had given him was not a gift. It was a target.

"I have nothing," Jie said, and the lie tasted like copper on his tongue.

Long Yin's eyes narrowed. "The Trials cannot begin without the decree. If you hold it, you must speak it. If you have destroyed it—"

"I would never."

"Then produce it."

Jie felt the thing pulse behind his heart. Felt it push against his ribs like a caged animal trying to escape. He did not know what it was. He did not know what it wanted. But he knew, with the certainty of a boy who had learned to survive through silence, that showing it would kill him.

"I will speak it," he said, "when the time is right."

"Your judgment is not trusted."

"Father trusted it."

"Father is dead."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Long Yin had spoken them without ceremony, without grief. Just fact. Just the truth of a world that had lost its center.

Jie looked at his brothers. Long Yin, coiled and patient. Long Feng, flames banked but ready. Long Shan, clouds swirling in agitation. Long Xu, watching from shadows. The eighth brother, Long Yi, who had not spoken a word in Jie's presence since they were children. And the ninth, Long Bo, who had taken the form of a great turtle, his shell inscribed with maps of all the realms.

None of them would help him. None of them had ever helped him.

"I will speak the decree," Jie said again, "when I understand what it means."

"You understand nothing," Long Yin said. "You are a child playing at dragon. You wear mortal skin because you cannot hold our form. You speak mortal words because you have not learned our tongue. You are not one of us, little brother. You never were."

The words struck like physical blows. Jie felt them land. Felt them settle into the spaces where his mother's hands used to rest.

"I am the Emperor's son."

"You are the Emperor's mistake."

Long Feng laughed. Long Shan's thunder rumbled in agreement. Even Long Xu, the quiet one, allowed a shadow of a smile to cross his indistinct features.

Jie turned away from them.

He walked to the edge of the arena and looked down at the mortal world. Somewhere down there, in the blue and green, his mother's village still stood. The house where she had raised him. The fields where she had taught him to grow rice. The river where she had shown him how to catch fish with his hands.

She had not been a dragon. She had been human. And she had loved him anyway.

"I will compete in the Trials," Jie said, his voice carrying across the arena despite the wind. "I will learn the decree. And when I understand it, I will speak it."

"You will not survive the first test," Long Yin said.

"Perhaps not."

"And yet you still try?"

Jie turned back. Met his eldest brother's eyes. Saw nothing there but cold calculation and the weight of centuries.

"Yes," he said. "I try. Because that is what my mother taught me. Not to win. To try."

Long Yin's coils tightened. His scales caught the light and threw it back in patterns of judgment. "Then let the Trials begin. But know this, little brother. If you hold the decree and you do not share it, you are not merely disobedient. You are treasonous. And treason carries a single punishment."

The arena grew cold.

"Death," Long Yin said.

Jie felt the thing pulse behind his heart again. Felt it push. Felt it burn.

And for the first time, he understood that his father had not given him a gift.

He had given him a weapon.

"You have three days," Long Yin continued. "Three days before the first trial. Use them to prepare. Or use them to say goodbye to your mortal attachments. It makes no difference to me."

He uncoiled and rose into the sky, his jade body catching the light as he ascended. The other brothers followed. Long Feng with a trail of fire. Long Shan with a rumble of thunder. Long Xu dissolving into shadow. Long Yi and Long Bo drifting after like afterthoughts.

Jie stood alone in the arena.

The mortal world spun below him. The sky stretched above him. And somewhere behind his heart, his father's final secret burned like a coal waiting to catch flame.

He did not know what the decree said.

He did not know why the Emperor had given it to him instead of to Long Yin.

But he knew one thing with absolute certainty.

His brothers would kill him for it.

And they would be right to try.

---

Far below, in the mortal village where Jie had been born, a woman with silver hair and eyes like winter frost looked up at the sky. She saw the dragons departing. Saw the trail of fire and shadow and storm.

She had been waiting for this day for sixteen years.

"Finally," she whispered, and her voice carried the weight of oaths older than the Celestial Empire. "The Emperor is dead. The sons are scattered. And the key walks free."

She reached into her robe and pulled out a scale. Black. Cracked. Thrumming with power that should not exist in mortal hands.

"You owe me a debt, Dragon King," she said to the empty air. "And I have come to collect."

The scale began to glow.

The sky began to darken.

And somewhere in the Celestial Arena, Jie felt a shift in the threads of fate. A pull. A warning.

He did not know what was coming.

But he knew, with the certainty of a boy who had learned to survive through silence, that the Trials were not the only danger he faced.

Someone else was playing the game now.

And they had been playing it far longer than he had.

title: The Weight of a Mortal's Tears
content:
The morning sun painted the Cloud-Span Peak in shades of amber and rose. Long Jie sat cross-legged on the edge of the Celestial Bridge, watching the mortal world sprawl beneath him like a tapestry of jade and gold. His brothers had already departed—each to their assigned starting points across the Nine Realms.

He should have been planning. Studying the trial rules. Sharpening his claws.

Instead, he watched a farmer below guide an ox through rice paddies. The man moved slowly, deliberately, his body bent with decades of labor. A mortal life. Forty, maybe fifty years if the gods were kind. Then dust.

Jie's tail curled around his ankle. Such fragile creatures. And yet...

"You're still here."

He didn't turn. He knew that voice—the rustle of silk robes, the weight of centuries behind each syllable. His mother, Lady Lian, the Dragon Emperor's third consort. The only one who'd ever called him by his birth name instead of "the runt."

"The trials don't start until sunset," Jie said. "I have time."

"You have six weeks." She settled beside him, her silver hair catching the light. In her mortal aspect, she looked no older than thirty. Jie knew she'd seen seven hundred winters. "Six weeks to complete three trials across three realms. Your brothers have already begun."

"Let them rush." Jie picked at a loose scale on his forearm. "Speed isn't everything."

"No." His mother's voice softened. "But neither is hiding."

He looked at her sharply. "I'm not hiding."

"You're watching mortals plow fields when you should be forging strategy." She gestured at the world below. "What do you see down there?"

Jie hesitated. "Small lives."

"Soon to be shorter ones." Lady Lian's eyes, the color of aged bronze, met his. "The Jade Emperor grows impatient with the Dragon Throne's vacancy. He's given your father's spirit one hundred days to choose an heir through you nine. After that..." She trailed off.

"After that, what?"

"He'll appoint a Celestial Dragon from the Heavenly Court. Your bloodline loses the throne forever."

The words hit like a physical blow. Jie's claws dug into the stone. The Dragon Throne had belonged to their family for ten thousand years. Ten thousand years of his ancestors breathing fire across the stars, shaping mountains, commanding storms. To lose it because his father couldn't pick a favorite son...

"Then I'll win," Jie said flatly.

"Will you?" His mother stood, brushing dust from her robes. "Your eldest brother commands the Eastern Seas. Your second brother bends volcanoes to his will. Your third brother can shatter mountains with a whisper." She paused. "You can talk to farmers."

"I can do more than—"

"Can you?" Lady Lian's voice cut like a blade. "Then prove it. The first trial begins in the mortal realm. Your starting point is the village of Twin Bridges, three days' flight east." She turned to leave, then stopped. "One more thing. Your seventh brother, Long Feng, chose the same starting point."

Jie's blood went cold. Feng. The brother who'd once broken his wing for flying too close to the Imperial Sun. The brother who'd laughed while Jie fell three thousand miles into the Sea of Sorrow.

"Why would he choose a mortal village?"

"He's not there for the trial." His mother's expression betrayed nothing. "He's there for you."

---

The village of Twin Bridges sat in a valley between two weeping cliffs, connected by ancient stone archways that had stood since before the First Dragon War. Jie landed at dusk, shifting to his mortal form to avoid drawing attention. Taller in this skin. Less conspicuous.

The streets were empty.

Not sleeping-empty. Wrong-empty. The kind of empty that made the hairs on your arms stand up. Doors hung open. A child's doll lay abandoned in the mud. The smell of smoke clung to everything.

Jie's hand went to the dagger at his belt. "Hello?"

Silence.

He moved through the village center, past the well with its cracked stone lip, past the temple with its shattered dragon statue. A cat darted across his path, fur bristling. It looked at him with wild eyes, then vanished into the shadows.

"Jie."

The voice came from behind him. He turned slowly.

Long Feng stood at the entrance to the village square, dressed in black robes embroidered with golden flames. His brother was beautiful—they all were, born of dragon blood—but Feng's beauty was the kind that promised pain. Sharp cheekbones. Eyes like hot coals. A smile that never touched his eyes.

"Seventh brother." Jie kept his voice neutral. "Shouldn't you be starting the trial?"

"I already finished." Feng stepped closer. The temperature around them rose. "The first test is a question, little brother. 'What do mortals fear most?' I gave my answer. The trial master was... impressed."

"What did you answer?"

Feng's smile widened. "Me."

The fire hit before Jie could react. A wall of crimson flame that consumed the square, melting the cobblestones, turning the well to steam. Jie threw himself sideways, rolling behind what remained of the temple wall. The heat was unbearable. His scales, even in mortal form, began to blacken.

"Come out, little brother." Feng's voice echoed through the inferno. "Father always favored you. The runt who couldn't even breathe fire properly. But he said you had 'potential.'" He laughed, and the sound was like grinding bones. "I've always wanted to see what that potential looks like when it burns."

Jie pressed himself against the stone. His mind raced. He couldn't match Feng in power. Couldn't outfly him. Couldn't—

The doll. The one in the mud.

He looked down. The child's doll lay at his feet, half-melted by the heat. A crude thing, sewn from rags and straw. A mortal child's treasure.

---

Jie had watched that farmer for three hours this morning. Watched him guide his ox, wipe sweat from his brow, pause to share his lunch with a stray dog. The man had nothing. No magic. No power. No thousand-year lifespan.

And yet he'd lived. Every day. Choosing to wake up, to work, to care for things smaller than himself.

Mortals didn't fear dragons because dragons were strong.

They feared dragons because dragons couldn't understand them.

Jie stood up.

"I'm here, brother."

The flames parted as Feng stepped through, his hands wreathed in fire. "Good. I was worried you'd run."

"I'm not running." Jie walked toward him, past the burning buildings, past the melting stone. "I'm answering the question."

Feng's eyes narrowed. "What?"

"The first trial. What do mortals fear most?" Jie stopped ten feet from his brother. The heat was agony. His skin blistered. His lungs screamed. "It's not you. It's not any of us. They fear being forgotten."

"Pretty words." Feng raised his hand, fire gathering in his palm. "But words won't—"

"Look around you." Jie didn't flinch. "This village. These people. They built bridges between cliffs. They carved temples into mountains. They raised children who will raise children who will raise children." He met his brother's gaze. "A dragon destroys a village. In a hundred years, the village is rebuilt. But a mortal idea—a song, a story, a bridge between two cliffs—that lasts forever."

Feng's hand trembled. The fire flickered.

"You think they'll remember you?" Feng whispered. "The runt who couldn't win?"

"No." Jie smiled. "But they'll remember the story of a boy who stood in fire and didn't burn. And that story will outlive both of us."

The flame in Feng's hand died.

For a long moment, they stood in the burning village, brothers separated by centuries of resentment and a single moment of understanding. Then Feng laughed—a real laugh this time, bitter and broken.

"Father was right about you." He turned away. "The trial master is in the temple at the valley's edge. Don't keep him waiting."

"And you?"

Feng glanced back. His eyes were wet. "I have other brothers to visit. We have six weeks, little brother. Try to survive them."

He vanished in a column of smoke.

Jie stood alone in the ashes. His body screamed with pain. His heart raced with fear. But beneath it all, something new stirred—a warmth that had nothing to do with fire.

He walked toward the valley's edge, toward the temple, toward the first answer of many.

Behind him, a child's doll lay in the mud, untouched by flame.

And in the shadows of a burning house, a young girl watched him go, her hand pressed to her mouth, her eyes wide with wonder.

title: The Mortal's Bargain
content:
The morning air tasted of smoke and river mud. Long Jie crouched at the edge of the fishing village, watching three brothers haul nets through the shallows. Their backs bent like old bamboo under the weight. One man's hands bled through the rope wrappings.

Jie had been here three days. Long enough to know their names. Long enough to watch them starve.

"Little lord," a voice called from behind. He turned to find Old Chen, the village elder, leaning on a staff carved from driftwood. "You've been watching my people like a hawk watches mice. What do you want?"

The old man's eyes held no fear. That was unusual. Most mortals trembled when they sensed dragon blood.

"I want to understand," Jie said.

"Understand what? That we're poor? That the river gives us nothing but grief?" Old Chen spat into the mud. "Your kind took our best fishing grounds three moons ago. Said the dragon prince needed them for his trials."

Jie's stomach tightened. "Which prince?"

"The one with the shell arm. The big one."

Long Ao. His fifth brother had always been ruthless, but this—stealing from mortals during the Ascension Trials—was against the old emperor's laws. Then again, their father was dead. Who would enforce them?

"The trials don't require stealing," Jie said carefully. "Each prince must complete a series of tests. Conquering territory isn't one of them."

Old Chen laughed. It sounded like stones grinding together. "Tell that to the prince who burned three boats last week when the fishermen refused to leave."

Heat crawled up Jie's neck. His father had built the Nine Realms on balance—dragon and mortal, spirit and flesh, power and restraint. What his brothers were doing now wasn't conquest. It was butchery.

"Show me where he's camped."

Old Chen studied him for a long moment. Whatever he saw made him nod slowly. "Follow the river north. You'll find his fortress in the Serpent's Bend. But little lord—" The old man's hand caught Jie's wrist. His grip was surprisingly strong. "If you're going to challenge him, do it properly. My people have suffered enough."

Jie pulled free gently. "I'm not here to challenge anyone. I'm here to learn."

The words tasted hollow, even to him.

---

The Serpent's Bend was exactly where Old Chen had said it would be. A fortress of packed earth and stolen timber rose from the riverbank, its walls lined with dragon-lanterns that burned with hungry blue fire. Jie counted twelve guards at the gate. Each wore the crimson armor of the Fifth Prince's personal legion.

He didn't bother with stealth. He walked straight up the main road, past the hovels of displaced villagers, past the drying racks stripped of fish. The guards saw him coming. They did nothing.

"The youngest prince," one said, his voice flat. "We were told you might come."

"Were you?" Jie stopped ten paces from the gate. "And what were you told to do?"

The guards exchanged glances. The speaker shrugged. "Let you pass. The Fifth Prince wants to see you."

That was unexpected. And dangerous. Jie had expected resistance, not invitation. He stepped through the gate anyway.

Long Ao's camp was a study in excess. Silk tents draped with gold thread. Braziers burning imported incense. A dozen mortal women in chains, their wrists bound with spirit-lock cord, kneeling by the cooking fires. Jie's scales rippled beneath his skin at the sight.

His brother sat on a throne of lashed-together boat hulls, cracking river clams with his bare hands. The shell-arm—a grotesque growth of hardened chitin that covered his entire right side—glittered in the firelight. When he saw Jie, he smiled.

"Little brother. Come to beg for scraps?"

"I came to ask you to stop."

Ao's laughter boomed across the camp. "Stop? The trials have barely begun. Father's throne won't claim itself." He threw a cracked shell at Jie's feet. "What would you have me do? Sit in a cave and meditate like you? Grow weak on mortal philosophy?"

"I would have you remember the law."

"The law is dead. Father is dead." Ao leaned forward. "You're the one who always wanted to be human, weren't you? The little dragon who played with mortal children. Who cried when they died of old age." His voice dropped to a sneer. "I'm doing these people a favor. I'll rule this realm eventually. Better they learn obedience now."

Jie's hands curled into fists. His dragon-fire stirred in his chest, hot and hungry. But he forced it down.

"If you want the throne so badly," he said, "prove yourself worthy of it. The trials measure strength, yes. But also wisdom. Justice."

Ao stood. The motion sent his guards scrambling. He walked toward Jie until they were nose to nose. His breath smelled of rot and brine.

"Let me tell you something our father never taught you," Ao said. "Justice is a word mortals invented because they're too weak to take what they want. Dragons don't need it."

"If that's true, then why don't you kill me now?"

The question hung in the air. Ao's shell-arm twitched.

"Because you're entertaining," he said finally. "And because I want you to watch when I win. I want you to know that your precious mortal lessons meant nothing."

He turned and walked back to his throne. "Get out of my camp, little brother. Go learn to fish. Maybe you'll catch enough to feed a village someday."

Jie didn't move. The chains on the mortal women caught the firelight. He could smell their fear, sharp and bitter.

"The fishermen," he said. "Let them return to their river."

Ao paused. "And why would I do that?"

"Because I'll give you something in return."

The Fifth Prince turned slowly. His eyes gleamed with interest. "What could you possibly have that I want?"

Jie reached into his robe and pulled out a jade pendant—the one their father had given him on his naming day. It held a single drop of the old emperor's blood, sealed in crystal. Ao's breath caught.

"The Dragon's Tear," he whispered. "You'd trade that? For a few mortal fishermen?"

"They're not a few. They're an entire village. And yes."

Ao laughed again, but this time there was no humor in it. "You're more foolish than I thought. That pendant could buy you protection. A place in the trials. A chance."

"I know."

"Then you know I'll use it against you."

"I know."

Ao snatched the pendant from Jie's hand. The warmth of their father's blood faded from Jie's chest, replaced by cold. "Fine. The fishermen can return. But if you come near my camp again, I'll have you chained next to the women."

Jie turned and walked away. Behind him, he heard Ao barking orders, heard the clatter of chains being released. He didn't look back.

---

The village was quiet when he returned. The fishermen were gathering their nets, their faces a mixture of relief and suspicion. Old Chen stood at the water's edge, staring at the river.

"It's done," Jie said. "Your people can fish again."

The old man didn't turn. "At what cost?"

"Nothing I couldn't afford."

"Lies." Old Chen's voice cracked. "I saw you give him the pendant. The one you wore when you arrived. The one with dragon blood inside."

Jie said nothing.

"That was your father's," the old man continued. "Your birthright. Your protection. And you traded it for strangers."

"You asked me to help."

"I asked you to fight. There's a difference."

Jie felt something break inside him. A thin, brittle thing that had held him together since his father's funeral. "I can't fight him. He's stronger. Faster. He has an army and a shell-arm and a hundred years of training that I never had because I was too busy learning to read human poetry."

"So you gave up?"

"No." Jie's voice hardened. "I made a choice. The pendant was power, but it was my father's power. Not mine. If I'm going to win these trials, I need to find my own."

Old Chen finally turned. His eyes were wet.

"You're a strange dragon, little lord."

"So I've been told."

"Don't mistake that for weakness."

Jie looked at the river. The water was gray and churning, carrying debris from upstream. Somewhere beyond the bend, the Serpent's Bend, his brother was laughing with his stolen pendant. Somewhere beyond that, seven other brothers were scheming and killing and burning their way toward the throne.

And here he was. In a fishing village. With nothing but his wits and the smell of smoke in his lungs.

"I need to learn," he said quietly. "Your people have survived dragons for centuries. You've been pushed, burned, stolen from. But you're still here. How?"

Old Chen was silent for a long time. Then he reached into his robe and pulled out a small wooden carving—a dragon with nine heads, each one chipped and faded.

"This," he said, placing it in Jie's hand, "is the reason. Every village in this realm has one. We carve them when a dragon threatens us. We pray to them. We remind ourselves that even the mightiest serpent was once a worm."

Jie turned the carving over. It was crude. Childish. But there was something in its imperfection that made his chest ache.

"Keep it," Old Chen said. "And learn this lesson: mortals don't survive dragons because we're strong. We survive because we're cunning. Because we know when to fight, when to flee, and when to give up something precious to keep something more precious safe."

"You just taught me what I gave away."

"No." The old man smiled, and for a moment he looked like a dragon himself. "I taught you what you gained."

Jie closed his fingers around the carving. The wood was warm.

"Thank you," he said.

"Don't thank me yet. You still have eight brothers to face." Old Chen pointed at the river. "And one of them is coming."

Jie turned. A boat was approaching from the north—sleek, black, flying a banner he didn't recognize. A single figure stood at the prow.

The figure raised a hand. Fire bloomed in her palm.

His sister, Long Mei. The Seventh Princess.

She was supposed to be dead.

---

The boat slid onto the mud bank. Mei stepped ashore with the grace of a hunting cat. Her robes were torn, her hair singed, her left arm wrapped in bloody bandages. But her eyes burned with dragon-fire.

"Little brother," she said, her voice hoarse. "I need your help."

"What happened to you?"

"Third brother happened." She spat blood into the river. "He ambushed me at the Jade Pass. Took my cultivation core. Left me for dead."

Jie's blood ran cold. Without a cultivation core, a dragon couldn't gather qi. Couldn't transform. Could barely survive.

"How are you still alive?"

Mei's smile was terrible. "I made a bargain with a mortal witch. She gave me a new core. But it's unstable. It's killing me."

Jie stared at her. His sister, who had once thrown him off a cliff for touching her scrolls. Who had laughed when he broke his wing. Who had never once shown him kindness.

And now she was standing in front of him, bleeding and

title: The Weight of Jade
content:
The blood of the morning sun painted the eastern peaks crimson when Long Jie found the old woman.

She sat at the crossroads where the mortal road met the spirit path, her back bent like a question mark against the weathered stone marker. A basket of herbs rested beside her, their leaves still wet with dew. Her hands moved with the practiced rhythm of someone who had spent decades weaving—except she held no thread.

Jie watched from the shadow of a pine. His dragon senses told him she was human. Completely, unremarkably human. No cultivation. No spirit affinity. Just flesh and bone and the quiet patience of someone who had learned to wait.

"You're blocking my sun, boy."

Her voice cracked like dry bamboo. Jie stepped forward, letting the morning light fall across his face. He had learned to read reactions—the widening of eyes, the sharp intake of breath, the way mortals' hands would reach for talismans or prayer beads. This woman did none of these things. She simply looked at him the way one looked at an interesting rock.

"You're one of them," she said. Not a question.

"One of what?"

"Dragon sons." She spat to the side. "I can smell the arrogance on you. Like burnt incense and spoiled milk."

Jie's tail flicked involuntarily. In the Celestial Palace, such disrespect would earn a mortal a hundred years of bad fortune. But this was not the Celestial Palace. This was the borderlands of Jiang Province, where the soil was red with iron and the people were harder than the stone they built their homes from.

"I am searching for a master," he said. "Someone who can teach me—"

"Teach you what?" The old woman laughed, a sound like grinding pebbles. "How to sit pretty on a throne? How to look down at the little people and pretend you understand their suffering?"

Jie felt heat rise in his chest. Third Brother would have incinerated this woman where she sat. Seventh Brother would have turned her to ice. But Jie was not his brothers. He was the runt. The failure. The one who looked too human to be a proper dragon.

"I want to learn how mortals survive," he said.

The old woman stopped laughing.

For a long moment, neither of them moved. A crane called somewhere in the mist. The wind carried the scent of rice paddies and cooking fires from a village Jie couldn't see.

"Survive?" The old woman's eyes narrowed. "You want to learn survival from creatures who live barely seventy years? Who break like dry twigs in a storm?"

"Yes."

"Why?"

Jie thought of his father's empty throne. The silence that had fallen over the Celestial Palace. The way his brothers had looked at him during the funeral—not with grief, but with calculation. They saw him as a competitor. A threat to be eliminated. They did not see him at all.

"Because my brothers think mortals are weak," Jie said. "And I think they're wrong."

The old woman studied him. Her eyes were the color of river stones, grey and depthless. Jie had the uncomfortable feeling that she could see through his skin, into the tangle of scales and bones and doubt that made up his true form.

"Come," she said, rising with a grunt. "If you want to learn about survival, you'll start by carrying my herbs."

---

The village was called Three Streams Crossing, named for the three rivers that met at its heart. It was a small place—perhaps fifty families, a few terraced fields, one inn that served rice wine so weak even mortals complained. The houses were built of mud and straw, their roofs patched with tiles of different colors. Chickens scratched in the dirt. Children ran barefoot through the streets.

And everyone stared at Jie.

He had tried to hide his dragon features. Wrapped his tail around his waist beneath his robes. Kept his horns veiled with a simple glamour. But something about him still marked him as other—perhaps the way he moved, too fluid for human joints. Perhaps the gold flecks in his eyes that no mortal possessed.

"Don't meet their gaze," the old woman said, not looking back. "You'll spook them worse than a fox in the hen house."

Jie lowered his eyes. The herbs smelled of ginger and something bitter. The basket was heavier than it should have been, as if the plants themselves resisted being carried.

They stopped at a house on the edge of the village. A young woman sat in the doorway, her face swollen with weeping. She held a bundle of cloth in her arms, and from it came the thin, reedy cry of an infant.

"Old Mother Chen," the woman said, relief flooding her voice. "The fever came back. He won't stop crying."

"Let me see."

The old woman—Mother Chen, apparently—took the bundle with surprising gentleness. The infant's face was flushed, his tiny fists beating at the air. Mother Chen pressed a finger to his forehead, then his chest. Her lips moved in a whisper Jie could not hear.

"Get me water from the third well," she said. "Not the first, not the second. The third."

The young woman ran.

Jie stood in the doorway, feeling useless. In the Celestial Palace, illness was a minor inconvenience. A word from a healer, a sip of immortal wine, and even the most stubborn fever would flee. But here, in this mud-and-straw house, a child's life hung by a thread no one could see.

"You're wondering why I don't just heal him," Mother Chen said without turning.

"I was wondering why you don't use spiritual energy."

She laughed again, that grinding-pebble sound. "Spiritual energy. You think it grows on trees? You think every village has a cultivator willing to waste power on a peasant child?"

"You're not a cultivator."

"No, boy. I'm a woman who knows which herbs work and which ones don't. I know that the third well draws from a deeper spring. I know that this child's fever comes from the mother's grief, not from any sickness in the air." She looked at him. "These are the things you cannot learn from meditation and breathing exercises."

The young woman returned with a clay pot of water. Mother Chen mixed herbs, crushed them between her fingers, made a paste that she spread on the infant's chest. The crying slowly stopped. The flush faded from his cheeks.

"There," Mother Chen said. "He'll sleep now. Keep him warm, but not too warm. Feed him only your milk, nothing else."

The young woman nodded, tears streaming down her face. She tried to press coins into Mother Chen's hand, but the old woman refused.

"Save it for the winter," she said. "I don't need silver."

They left the house. The sun had climbed higher, burning away the mist. Jie followed Mother Chen through the village, past the rice paddies where farmers bent over their work, past the grinding mill where a donkey walked in endless circles, past the temple where an old priest swept dust that would never stay swept.

"You want to know the secret of mortal survival?" Mother Chen said finally.

"Yes."

"We give up."

Jie stopped walking.

"Every day," Mother Chen continued, "we wake up knowing we will die. Knowing that our children will die. Knowing that everything we build will crumble, that every name we carve in stone will be worn away by rain and time. And we get up anyway. We plant rice knowing we might not live to harvest it. We love knowing we will lose. We hope knowing that hope is a fool's game."

She turned to face him. Her eyes were not kind. They were the eyes of someone who had seen too much to be kind.

"That is what your brothers will never understand. They think power is everything. They think immortality makes them strong. But strength is not living forever. Strength is choosing to live when everything tells you to die."

Jie felt something shift in his chest. Not his heart—something deeper. Something that had been sleeping since his father's death.

"And the Trials?" he asked. "You think I can win them with this... mortal wisdom?"

Mother Chen smiled. It was not a comforting smile.

"Boy, I don't think you'll win anything. I think your brothers will tear each other apart trying to claim a throne that will burn whoever sits on it. And I think you'll be caught in the fire, just like everyone else."

She reached into her basket and pulled out a jade pendant. It was carved in the shape of a coiled dragon, its eyes closed, its body wrapped around itself in an endless loop.

"Your father gave this to me. Forty years ago, when he was still young and foolish and thought he could understand mortals by sleeping with them."

Jie's blood turned cold.

"What?"

"Your father had a child with a mortal woman. A daughter. She died in childbirth, but the child lived." Mother Chen's voice was flat, emotionless. "That child grew up. Had children of her own. Their children had children. And now, somewhere in this province, there is a family that carries dragon blood in their veins—blood they don't even know about."

She pressed the pendant into Jie's hand. The jade was warm, pulsing with a familiar energy. His father's energy.

"Your brothers don't know," Mother Chen said. "And if they find out, they will kill every last one of them. Because a half-dragon heir has no place in the Celestial Palace. And a full-blooded dragon emperor cannot tolerate competition."

Jie stared at the pendant. His hand trembled.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you asked the right question." Mother Chen turned and walked away, her basket swinging. "And because I want to see what you do with the answer."

She disappeared around a corner. Jie stood alone in the dusty street, holding his father's jade, feeling the weight of a secret that could destroy everything.

Behind him, someone cleared their throat.

He turned. A young man stood there—perhaps twenty years old, with the same grey eyes as Mother Chen. He was thin, dressed in patched robes, his hands callused from labor.

"You're Long Jie," the young man said. "I've been looking for you."

"And you are?"

The young man smiled. It was a familiar smile. Jie had seen it in the mirror every day for the past three hundred years.

"My name is Long Sheng," he said. "I believe we're brothers."
